**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

---

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient stone walls. The sea roars in fury, and the lighthouse beams flicker like a dying man's last breath. I’ve spent five long years in this lonely tower, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and now, as the storm approaches, I feel the weight of the world on my shoulders.

You were just a child when you left, not even ten years old. I remember the day you ran from the house, tears streaming down your face, your little hands clutching a bundle of clothes and a toy horse. You said you wanted to be “free,” that you didn’t belong in this life. I tried to reason with you, but you were too young, too stubborn, and too hurt to listen. I never meant to push you away, but in my heart, I feared what I had become.

I’ve kept the lighthouse, not for the glory, but because it’s all I have left. I’ve seen the world change—ships come and go, the sky shifts from blue to black, and the stars have faded from the sky. But I’ve never forgotten you. I’ve dreamed of you, of the way you used to laugh, the way you’d run to me with your little hands, asking about the

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now, beneath the flickering light of the old lamp that has stood watch over these cliffs for more than thirty years. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that makes me tremble. I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet, as this storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

I know not if you will read this, for I may not live to see the dawn. But if you do, I beg you, my dear, to forgive me. I have been a fool, and I have let my pride blind me to the truth of what I have lost. You were always my light, Eliza, and I have let it fade from my life.

I have kept the lamp lit, as I always have, but I have forgotten that there is another light that must be kept alive—your light, your heart, your spirit. I have been too busy watching the sea to see the world through your eyes. I have been too proud to admit that I have missed you more than the tides miss the shore.

I pray that you have found peace, that you have found love and purpose in your own way. I pray that you have not been shaped by the same darkness that has kept me from you. I know not if you still hold any affection for me, but I would give anything, even my life, to have

**The Lighthouse, Dunmore Head**  
**April 13th, 1898**  

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it will find you in a far greater state than I expect to be in myself. I write with a quill that trembles more from the cold than from any sentiment, and with a hand that has grown unsteady with age and the weight of years.

You were always the cleverest child, Eleanor, and I must confess I never did understand your choosing to leave this place—this *life*—for the city. I suppose I was too rigid in my ways, too set in my ways, and you were always the one who longed for more than the sea and the light. But I have missed you, child. I have missed you more than I can say.

The storm is coming. I have seen the sky darken like the day you left, and the wind howls as if it too remembers the day you shut the door behind you. I have been keeping the light burning all these years, though I have no one left to guide. The sea is restless, and I fear it may take more than just the lighthouse.

I have not seen the mainland in five years, and I do not expect to see it again. The lighthouse has been my home, my duty, my burden. But tonight, I fear it may

*The wind howls like a wounded beast through the narrow stone corridors of the lighthouse. I've lit the lamp, though I know it's not enough to keep the darkness at bay. My hands are steady, though my heart is not. I've written this letter with trembling fingers, for I know I may not be here to see the dawn. I must speak now, before the storm swallows me whole.*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I've been a lighthouse keeper for nearly as long. You were only twelve when you left, and I've never stopped wondering what became of you. I've kept the old lantern, the one you used to help me with when you were a child. It's been a faithful companion, though I've had to keep it lit all the time now—no one else to watch the sea, no one else to see the light.

The sea has been restless lately, and the storm is coming. I've been watching the clouds all day, and I fear it's not just the wind that will take me. I've heard the stories, the whispers of the sea, and I've always thought they were just old sailor tales. But tonight, I'm not so sure.

I've packed a small bundle—some of your old things, a few letters I've saved, and a bit of food. I don't know if you'll find them, but I have to

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It is the eve of a tempest most dire, and the wind howls like the ghosts of our past. I have kept this letter penned for some time, yet the storm's approach has made me feel as though time itself is in a frenzy, and I must speak before the waves claim me.

You have not written to me in five long years, and I have not dared to write to you, for fear that the words might not reach you, or worse, that they might be misconstrued. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the sorrow in my soul, I find I must try once more.

I have seen the world change around me, Eliza. The lighthouse has stood for many years, a sentinel of the coast, but the world beyond has shifted. I have watched the ships pass, the fishermen return, and the children of the village grow into men and women. Yet in all this, I have seen no sign of you. I wonder if you still live, or if the sea has taken you as it has taken so many others.

I have not seen you since the day you left, and I cannot say if it was your choice or mine. I recall the last time we spoke, your voice was sharp with anger, and your eyes held a fire that I could

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it is more likely to find you in the midst of your own storm—be it of heart or circumstance. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have tried to forget the last words we exchanged, I find myself unable to let them rest. The sea has a way of tugging at the soul, and so too does the memory of you.

I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of something far greater pressing upon me. The skies have turned an ominous shade of iron, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The lighthouse, though sturdy, is not impervious to the fury of nature. I have spent my life watching the tides and the stars, but tonight, I fear the sea may take more than it gives.

You were always the child who questioned the world, Eleanor. You asked why the waves crashed against the rocks, why the stars shimmered in the night, and why we must endure so much pain for so little gain. I used to tell you that the light must shine, no matter the storm. But now, as I stand at the helm, I wonder if the light is not only for others—but for me.

I have not seen you in years, and I do not know if I will live to see you again. The lighthouse is my home

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I must write—though I know you may never read these words.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and I have kept your name in my heart like a lighthouse beam through the darkest night. I have not spoken to you, not so much for spite, but because I feared the words would break me. You were but a child when you left, and I could not bear to see you go. I thought I had done my duty, and yet… I have not seen you in all these years.

The lighthouse stands, though the lantern has not burned for weeks. I have grown old, my back bent, my bones like the stone I tend. The sea has taught me patience, and I have learned to wait. But tonight, the sky is black as a tomb, and the waves crash like the beating of a heart. I have not spoken to another soul in this place for many a day, and I have not heard the sound of your voice. I have not seen your face.

I have no cause to reproach you, for I know the world is cruel, and you were but a young woman seeking her own path. But I have not forgotten the day you left.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and in the company of those who love you. I write to you from the cliffs of this desolate shore, where the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars with a fury that shakes the very bones of the earth. I have not penned a word to you in five long years, and I fear the silence has grown heavier than the sea’s waves upon these stones. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the heavens weep with a vengeance, I find myself compelled to write, for I do not know if I shall see another dawn.

You were but a child when you left this house, and though I have watched the years pass with the patience of a man who has long since given up hope, I have not forgotten the day you fled. I do not blame you, Eleanor—no, I do not. I blame only the cruel winds of fate that drove you from my side. You were young, and your heart was heavy with dreams that I could not understand. You spoke of a world beyond these cliffs, of cities that shimmer in the sun and of lives that are not bound to the rhythm of the tides. I could not fathom such a place, and so I said nothing. But I watched, and I waited, and I prayed that time would mend what sorrow had torn.

Now, as I sit in the lantern room, the light flickering like

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since your last letter, and five years have been spent in the ceaseless vigil of this lighthouse, where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the wind sings dirges through the cracks of the stone.

You were but a girl when you left, and now you are a woman of years and silence. I have seen your name fade from the walls of our home, and the hearth where we once shared stories now burns cold. You have not written, nor have you come. I have not written to you, for I feared what words might bring—what truths, what regrets, what wounds.

But tonight, the heavens have turned their gaze upon this coast. The stars have been blotted out, and the sea, which has ever been my companion, now roars with a fury I have not seen since the day you left. The storm is coming, and it comes with a wrath that may not spare the land, nor the soul of a man who has stood alone in this tower for so long.

I have no physician, no comfort, no friend but the wind and the sea. I have no lamp to guide the ships, for the oil is low, and the wick is brittle. I have no food but the salt of the waves and the bread of my own hands

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, in the dim light of this lighthouse, with the howling wind outside and the sea roaring like a beast in pain. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may not spare us. Yet, I must write, for my hands tremble not with fear, but with the weight of what I have kept in my heart for five long years.

You were once my light, Eleanor. A beacon in my life, as bright and steady as the flame that guards this tower. But time, like the tides, has carried us apart. I have not spoken to you in five years, and though I have not written, I have thought of you often—more than I care to admit.

I know you do not forgive me. You have every right not to. I was blind, Eleanor, blind to the pain I caused you. I was too wrapped in my duty, my solitude, my pride. I let the sea speak for me when I should have spoken to you. I let the silence between us grow like the salt on these stones, hard and unyielding.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself questioning whether I will survive this night. I have always been the keeper of this lighthouse, the guardian of those who sail these waters. But tonight, I fear I may not be able to guard anything but my own breath.

I have no words to undo the hurt I have

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old stone tower. The sea roars with a fury I have never known, and the sky is a bruise of black and green, as though the heavens themselves weep for the world. I have not seen you in five years, Eliza, and I fear I may not see you again. But I must write, for I know not if I shall live to speak these words aloud.

You were once my light, child. Not the lantern that burns upon this cliff, but the warmth in my heart when the night was long and the cold pressed hard against my bones. I remember the days when you would climb up these steps with your little hands, and I would hold you close, as if you might never leave me. You were my joy, my hope, my everything. But time, as cruel as the sea, has taken much from me—my health, my peace, and, most cruelly, your love.

I have not written to you in years, and I have not spoken to you, for I feared the words would break me. I feared that if I called you, I might not be able to let you go. And so I let the years pass, and the silence grew, and I became a man who no longer knew how to love, only how to watch.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I daresay you may not have received it. I have been meaning to write for some time, yet the sea has been restless of late, and I have been too preoccupied with the demands of this old tower to find the time or the resolve. But now, with the storm on the horizon and the wind howling like a wounded beast, I feel the need to put pen to paper before the night consumes me entirely.

You may be surprised to learn that I have not been entirely idle these past five years. The lighthouse has been a faithful companion, and though I have never been one for company, it has kept me company in its own way. The light has been a constant presence, guiding ships through the dark, and I have found solace in its steady glow. It is strange, really, how one can grow fond of a thing that never speaks, yet never fails.

I have not written to you because I feared the words would not be enough, and I would only be left with the silence between them. You have always been a source of consternation to me, Eleanor, and I have not the heart to revisit the old wounds. But perhaps it is time. Perhaps the storm that threatens the coast will be a fitting metaphor for the tempest that has raged within me these past years.

I have heard from your brother, Thomas. He writes

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

---

**To My Dearest Elara,**

If you're reading this, then the storm has taken me—either that, or the sea has claimed me in its cruel, endless embrace. Either way, I write this with the last of my strength, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse, and the sea roiling like it's trying to swallow the very rocks beneath me.

Five years have passed since you left, and I've kept the door locked, not because I wanted to keep you out, but because I didn't want to keep you in. You were so young, so full of fire and ambition, and I... I was too stubborn to let you go. I told you I'd always be here, that this lighthouse was my home, my duty, my life. But I was wrong. You needed more than a man who could not change his ways. You needed a father who could let you go.

I've thought of you every night, every storm, every time the light flickered and I had to climb the tower to keep it burning. I've imagined you standing at the edge of the cliff, watching the waves, maybe even smiling at the horizon. I've imagined you returning, not as the girl I once knew, but as a woman with her own path, her own dreams. And I would have been proud, even if I never

My Dearest Eliza,

I sit by the fire, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, and I write to you with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow. Five years have passed since I last saw you, and though the sea may have carried you away, it has not carried my love for you with it. I have been a fool, Eliza, to let pride and silence drive the bond between us apart. I have been a lighthouse keeper, yes, but I have not been a father to you.

The storm is upon us now, a tempest such as I have not seen in my years of service. The waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very earth. I have seen many storms, but never one like this. I fear for my life, and yet, in this moment, I find myself thinking not of the sea, but of you. I have not seen you in so long, and I have not spoken to you, and I fear that I may never see you again. But if I do not, I would have you know that I have never stopped loving you.

You were but a child when you left, and I was a man too proud to say the words that I should have. I remember the day you left, the way you looked at me, and the way you turned away. I was too afraid to tell you the truth, too afraid that you would not understand. I thought that you would

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  
**April 12th, 1889**  

**My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old adversary, roars in its fury. I fear we may not see dawn. But I must write, for I have something to say before the light goes out—forever, perhaps.  

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and five years since I’ve dared to call you by name. I’ve kept the lighthouse, as you would have wanted, and I’ve kept the light burning, as you once said was the duty of a man who would not let the world be swallowed by darkness. But I’ve not kept the silence.  

I’ve not kept the pain.  

I’ve heard from the village, you know. They say you’ve taken a position in London, in some grand house, with a husband and children. They say you are happy. They say you’ve forgotten me. But I know better. I know the look in your eyes when you left, the way you turned your back as if I were a ghost. You did not forget me. You could not.  

But I have forgotten you.  

Or perhaps I have not. Perhaps I have only buried the memory deeper, like a shipwreck beneath the waves. I

*The ink is shaky, the paper yellowed with age, and the lamp burns low. I write with a steady hand, though my heart is a storm of its own. The sea howls like a beast, and the wind screams through the cracks of this old tower. I've been alone for too long, but tonight... tonight, I feel the weight of the world on my shoulders. I must say my farewells before the darkness takes me. Let me tell her the truth, the whole truth...*

I hope this letter finds you in better hands than mine. It has been five long years since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have tried to write, but the sea has been restless, and the storms have been cruel. I have been alone here, with only the lamp and the sea for company.

I know you think I have forgotten you. I know you think I have given up on us. But I have not. I have not forgotten the love we once had.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From the desolate cliffs of Gull's Hollow, 12th of November, 1887**

Five years have passed since you last set foot in this place, and I have not seen you since the day you left with that fine gentleman, Mr. Wren. I recall the look in your eyes—cold, resolute, as though you had left not merely a father, but a man. You spoke of duty, of a future beyond this bleak coast, and I, in my pride, allowed it. Yet I have since come to see that duty is a cruel master, and that the world is not as you believed.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have been a torment. I have not heard from you, nor seen your face, nor felt your hand. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, but it seems the light has not reached you. I have been a fool, I know, and I have been a father who failed. I have not been the man you deserved, nor the father you needed.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*To my dearest Elara,*

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes on you, and though the days have dragged by like a ship caught in a tempest, I’ve carried your memory with me like a lantern through the darkest night.

The wind howls now, and the sea roars with a fury that even the old lighthouse cannot contain. I’ve been here for over thirty years, watching the waves crash against these rocks, but tonight feels different. The sky is blacker than the deepest abyss, and the storm is coming with a vengeance. I’ve seen the sea rise, and I know it won’t be long before the waves break through the walls of this old place.

I’ve been thinking a lot lately, about the choices we made, the words we left unsaid. I know you blamed me for leaving, for not being there when you needed me most. But I had no choice, Elara. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I was bound to her service. I couldn’t abandon my post, not when the light was needed most.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, as I have kept thy memory, steadfast and unyielding. The light burns bright, as it has always done, and I have watched the waves crash against the rocks with a sorrow that no tide can wash away. I have prayed for thee, Eliza, in every storm that has passed, and I have wondered if thou wouldst ever return to this place, to this life, to this man who once loved thee more than the sea itself.

I know not if I shall see another dawn. The wind grows fiercer, the waves more relentless, and the storm seems to have no end. But if by some miracle I should survive, I would have thee know that I have never stopped loving thee. That I have never stopped hoping. That I have never stopped longing for the day when we might once again stand together, as we once were.

I have not spoken in five years, but I have never ceased to think of thee. I have kept the lighthouse, and I have kept the hope that one day, thou

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

*Porthgwen, Isle of Mynydd, 17th of October, 1889*  
*Midnight*

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old foe, roars in defiance of the heavens. The lantern’s light flickers—God alone knows how long it will hold. But I write now, for I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left this house, that stone hovel of a home, and with it, your heart. I have not heard from you, not a word, not a letter, not even a postcard from the village. You have cut yourself off from me, as you have cut yourself off from the world. I suppose I should not be surprised. You always did have your own way of things.

But I write not for the sake of remembrance, nor for the old wounds, but because I fear this storm may take me before I can say what I must.

You were a fine girl, Eliza. Bright as the light that guides the ships through the dark. You had a gift for words, a mind that danced with ideas and dreams. I used to think you would have made a fine writer, or a fine lady, or perhaps a fine wife. But you chose none of these

**To My Dearest Elara,**

I hope this letter finds you in better hands than mine. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and I fear the time may be running out. Tonight, a storm of such fury I have never known is gathering on the horizon, and I do not know if I will see dawn.

You were always the clever one, my child. I remember the day you first came to me with your sketches of the sea, your eyes alight with wonder. You were so full of questions, always asking why the waves came and went, why the stars shone so bright. I used to tell you stories of the old sailors and the ghosts who sailed with the tide. But I fear your heart has grown tired of such tales.

I have not written to you in so long, not because I do not care, but because I feared what I might say. You were always so quick to leave, to chase your dreams across the world. I thought I could not keep you here, not when you were so full of fire and purpose. But now, as I stand alone on this cliff, with the wind howling like a beast and the sea rising in fury, I wonder if I made a mistake.

You were not meant to be tied to this place, this life of salt and solitude. I know I was not the kind of father you needed. I was too old, too stubborn, too much of a lighthouse keeper

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I trust this missive finds you in good health, though I fear the winds may have carried your letters far beyond the reach of my humble abode. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I must confess, I have often found myself gazing upon the horizon, hoping to glimpse the familiar silhouette of your carriage or the crest of your father's estate. Yet, alas, the sea has been both a faithful companion and a cruel adversary, and I have not seen you since that fateful day when you left us behind.

The storm that now approaches is of a most formidable nature. The skies have darkened with an ominous hue, and the waves have grown with a wrath that dares to rival the fury of the heavens themselves. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for many a year, but I fear the tempest may well extinguish its glow before dawn. Should I not survive this night, I pray that you may find solace in the knowledge that I did not perish in vain. The lighthouse stands as a testament to my duty, and I have ever striven to keep the path safe for those who dare to traverse these perilous waters.

I have often thought of you in the quiet hours, when the wind howls like a mournful spirit and the sea sings its ancient ballads. I have imagined the life you now lead, the laughter of your children, the warmth of your hearth—

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I must confess that my own heart is heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long, silent years since our last exchange, and I find myself wondering if you still remember the way the sea used to shimmer at dusk, or if you’ve forgotten the sound of my voice—gruff, but ever so sincere.

I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the desperate need to speak, to be heard, to be known. The storm that gathers on the horizon is no mere tempest of wind and rain. It is a reckoning, a trial of spirit and endurance, and I fear that the night ahead may not be kind to any of us. I have spent my life standing firm against the elements, Eleanor, but even the sturdiest lighthouse may falter in the face of the divine.

You were once my greatest pride, my quiet hope for the future. I remember the way you would sit by the window, watching the waves, your eyes full of wonder and questions. You were always curious, always reaching for something more than the world could offer. I was too busy with the duties of the light, too wrapped up in the routines of this life to see that you were growing, changing, becoming someone else. **To My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heavy heart, and a hand that trembles, that I take up my quill to write to thee. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet, the memory of thy face lingers in my mind like a ghost upon the sea. I pray that this letter finds thee well, though I fear the winds may carry it far, or that the sea may claim it ere it reaches thy hands.

The storm is upon us, and I stand upon the cliff, the wind howling like a beast in torment. The waves rise high, and the lighthouse, my steadfast companion, groans beneath the strain. I have spent a lifetime watching the tides, and now, as the heavens weep, I wonder if I shall see the dawn. But I write not for myself, but for thee, my daughter.

I have not seen thy face in years, and yet I dream of it oft. I have heard tales of thee from the sailors who come to seek shelter, and I have heard of a woman who walks with purpose, with fire in her eyes, and with a heart that doth not bend to the will of others. I know not if these tales be true, but I would have thee know that I have not forgotten thee, nor the days we shared. Though we partook of bitter words, I have never ceased to love thee.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in better health and more peace than I, for I write to you with a heart heavy with both sorrow and resolve. Five long years have passed since our last correspondence, and though the tides of time have turned, I find myself still bound by the weight of our silence. I have not spoken your name in so many months, yet it lingers in my thoughts like the echo of a distant bell upon the cliffs. 

You were once my light, Eleanor, and now, in these dark hours of my twilight years, I find myself longing for the warmth of your presence more than ever. I have watched the sea with its ever-changing moods, and I have come to know that even the most steadfast lighthouse must sometimes falter in the face of the tempest. 

Tonight, a storm is coming—one such as I have not seen in many a year. The winds howl like the voices of the lost, and the waves rise like the fury of the heavens themselves. I have prepared the lamps, the ropes, the provisions, and the prayers, for I know that in such a night, one must be ready not only for the storm, but for the silence that follows. I have seen the clouds darken, and I fear that the sea may not be merciful.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write these words with a quill that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown like the salt upon my beard—hard, unyielding, and salted with regret.

You were a child of fire and wind, Eliza, and I, a man of stone and tide. You left with the wind, and I remained with the sea. I did not stop you, for I believed you had your own path to walk. But I did not know, not truly, that it would be a path of severance.

Now, as the storm gathers, I find myself in a place where the sky weeps and the sea howls. The lighthouse stands, as it always has, but its light grows dim. The lamps are failing, and the wind has taken the breath from my chest. I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

I have not spoken of you in these years, not because I have no words, but because I feared they would be too much. Too much to say, too much to undo. But now, as the night tightens its grip and the waves claw at the rocks, I find that my voice, though weathered, still holds a whisper of you.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, in the dim glow of the lighthouse, with the sea howling like a wounded beast and the wind tearing at the shutters as though it would tear the very soul from my chest. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written, not even once, since the day you left.

You were but a girl then, with eyes like the sea at dawn—clear, bright, and full of promise. I told you so, though I know you heard me, and yet you chose to leave, as if the world had grown too small for your dreams. You wanted the stars, Eleanor, and I could not give them to you. I was bound to this stone, to this light, to the endless watch of the horizon.

I have not forgiven you for leaving, nor have I forgotten the day you walked away, your back turned, your voice like a whisper of wind. But I have not forgotten the way your mother wept in the parlor, nor the way I told her that I would not beg you to stay. I would not be the man who begged. I would be the man who stood by the light, and let the sea take what it wished.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The night is thick with the scent of sea and storm, and the wind howls as if it knows the truth that lingers in the silence between us. I have not spoken your name in five years, not since the last time I saw you, and yet it is as if the wind itself carries your voice through the gales, and I must write, for I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, though I feel it sway more now than before. The sea, that old and relentless thing, grows restless. The waves have grown fierce, and the sky, a bruised shade of grey, hangs low over the horizon, as if it too is weeping. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight, I do not feel the light as I once did. It seems to me that the light is dimmer, and the sea more cruel. I wonder if it is the sea, or if it is I.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza. Even when the storms came and the sky wept, you were the calm in the chaos. You were the child who once climbed the stone steps with me, who laughed at the wind and the waves, who would press her small hand to my chest and say, “D

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I have been of late. I write to you not with the hope of mending the rift between us, but with the weight of a storm upon my soul and the sea howling like a beast in distress. You will not be surprised to learn that I have not spoken in five years, and I suspect you have not written. Yet, I feel the need to say something, though I fear my words may not reach you, or may not be worth the effort of reading.

The lighthouse is in a state of disrepair, as you might imagine. The wind has taken the roof tiles, and the sea has taken the light. The keeper’s quarters are damp, the lantern is dim, and the fire is but a flicker. I have been keeping the lamp burning, though, for the sake of the ships that might yet find their way here. It is a duty I have not taken lightly, though I confess I have not been the most diligent of keepers. I have grown weary, Eleanor. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I have come to know her well.
